claim: Tom Lodziak's central cue is to contact the ball more towards the handle where the bat is moving slower, using a similar action to the heavy-spin serve but without whipping the ball, and to keep the no-spin variant the same length and speed as the spin twin.
quote: contact the ball more towards the handle, where the bat is moving slower… use a similar action to your heavy spin serve. But don't whip the ball as much… a similar length and speed as your heavy spin serve

claim: Tom Lodziak's '4 simple serves for beginner players' video demonstrates backspin and no-spin together by changing bat angle at the last moment while keeping the same service motion.
quote: how to do four simple serves… a backspin serve, a topspin serve, a sidespin serve and a no spin serve… learn how to add different spins to the ball by changing your bat angle and swing trajectory

claim: PingSkills (Alois Rosario / Jeff Plumb) has a dedicated 'Backspin and No Spin Serve Variations' tutorial built around using the same motion to get attacking opportunities on the 3rd ball.
quote: get the backspin and no spin serve variations working for you… with an effective variation you will get a lot of attacking opportunities on the 3rd ball

claim: PingSkills' explicit cue is to contact the ball close to the handle of the blade because that part has moved through a smaller arc and so imparts less spin.
quote: you can contact the ball close to the handle of your blade, since that part of your blade has moved through a smaller arc and so you will impart less spin

claim: EmRatThich (PingSunday) frames deception as 'same motion, different contact point' and recommends using the same serve in different ways and different serves with the same action as the core training principle.
quote: change the contact point but keep the same serve motion, so with the same motion you can change the spin of your serve… train to use the same serve in different ways and different serves with the same action

claim: EmRatThich's forehand pendulum tutorial gives the concrete coordinate cue: contacting at the (1,2) or (2,2) position of the racket — i.e. near the handle — produces a no-spin floating serve from the same pendulum motion.
quote: contact the ball near the handle of the racket… contacting at the (1,2) or (2,2) position will make a no-spin, floating serve

claim: MLFM's 'Short Pendulum Serve Tutorial | No Spin' (uploaded 28 June 2020) is dedicated to the dead-ball variant and explicitly tells viewers to combine it with the backspin serve as the same-motion pair.
quote: I teach the dead ball serve in this video. Some tips to help with your dead ball serve include:* Contact the ball low* Combine with the backspin serve for be...

claim: MLFM's companion 'Short Pendulum Serve Tutorial | Backspin' (uploaded 12 April 2020) is the explicit spin twin to the no-spin tutorial, letting learners study the identical arm path side-by-side.
quote: I demonstrate how to do the short backspin pendulum serve. I teach:* How to hold the racket for this serve* The Stance* The Stroke* How to get the backspin*

claim: Community consensus on float-serve deception adds two practical cues beyond the contact-point rule: tap flat instead of brushing, and stomp the foot at contact to mask the softer ball-blade sound.
quote: contact the ball as close to the handle as possible when going for a float serve, and stomping your foot during the serve helps suppress the sound of the ball contacting the bat

claim: Tom Lodziak's 'Trick your opponents with service deception' video (with coach Craig Bryant) covers backspin/topspin/sidespin deception explicitly built around making the action look the same but produce opposite spin.
quote: your service action looks like topspin, but the spin is actually backspin… backspin deception, topspin deception and sidespin deception

claim: TableTennisDaily's 'FOOL Your Opponents: Learn to Change the Spin of Your Pendulum Table Tennis Serves' (4-min edit, 1 May 2025) is the most current dedicated tutorial on switching pendulum spin between backspin/sidespin/topspin/no-spin from one motion.
quote: In this 4 minute edit, learn how to change between backspin, sidespin, topspin and no spin variations for the pendulum table tennis serve.

claim: TableTennisDaily's longer 'Changing Spin on the Pendulum Serve' seminar (Mar 2020) explicitly teaches keeping the service action visually identical while swapping spins — closest match to the same-motion drill the parent needs.
quote: a short seminar-style lesson on how to change between backspin, sidespin and topspin on the pendulum serve - all while maintaining a service action...

claim: MLFM's 'Learning UNPREDICTABLE Backspin & No Spin Serve' (29 Nov 2019) is the explicit pair-tutorial covering both serves together as a deception package.
quote: Learning UNPREDICTABLE Backspin & No Spin Serve | MLFM Table Tennis Tutorial

claim: MLFM's 'FAST Pendulum Serve Tutorial | No Spin' (17 May 2020) cues mimicking the spin-serve motion at higher pace as the deception layer for the long no-spin variant.
quote: I teach you how to do the fast no spin pendulum serve in this video. My top tips for doing the fast no-spin are to:* Contact the ball low* Try to mimic your...
